narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into building a strong foundation for your legal career. Your family falls into the middle income bracket, and your early high school grades placed you in the 5th decile in Year 1, while Year 3 put you in the 3rd decile—showing some variability in your academic trajectory. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 42.7% chance that you would fail, which crossed the threshold for rejecting your application to protect the school's bar passage rates. This prediction wasn't random; it weighed several key aspects of your profile against patterns from thousands of past students. The two biggest factors pulling in opposite directions were your undergraduate GPA and your LSAT score. Your above-average GPA (stronger than the typical 3.21) was the most influential positive force, strongly pushing the model toward expecting you to pass the bar, as it signals consistent college-level performance. On the flip side, your LSAT score of 24—well below the average of 36.7—had the strongest negative pull, significantly raising concerns about bar exam readiness since it's a top predictor in these models. Your Year 1 decile ranking also helped a bit by nudging toward success compared to peers, while your middle family income and full-time status offered minor boosts. However, your lower Year 3 decile slightly tipped things toward higher failure risk. Compared to typical students, your GPA stands out positively, but the LSAT gap is what sets you apart in a challenging way, as most admitted applicants score much higher. To move forward, I recommend retaking the LSAT to boost that score, perhaps while strengthening your recent academic decile through additional coursework or prep programs tailored for bar success. Many students in similar situations have turned things around with focused improvement—your strong GPA shows you have the potential, so don't lose hope. We're here to support your next steps.
